title: Quantum Error Correction Exploiting Quantum Spatial Distribution and Gauge Symmetry
abstract: We explore what the integrated use of quantum spatial distribution (QSD), or more specifically, superposition of both spin and position states of particles, and gauge symmetry (GS) within stabilizer formalism provides for quantum error correction. The exploration employs $3+2$ particles on nested squares proposed in the companion letter (arXiv:2504.07941), where three of them encode Shor's nine-qubit code and the remaining two detect errors in this code through their spin state measurements (unlike the letter's quantum walk model, each particle evolves by gate operations acting exclusively on either its spin or position state). The first result is that the GS offers resilience against three types of noise acting on a particle: arbitrary decoherence of its spin or position state, and dephasing of both states, which partly or completely destroys its QSD. To show that, we formulate a noise model unifying the above noise and prove the correctability of this unified model under our error-correcting scheme. The second result is that QSD provides architectural flexibility allowing us to stack the error-correcting systems both vertically and horizontally. Indeed, we show implementations of the error detection (stabilizer measurement), logical Hadamard and Toffoli gates, and a quantum adder with the required interactions only between nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor particles.
summary: This paper introduces a quantum error correction scheme that utilizes both the spin and position states of particles (Quantum Spatial Distribution). By leveraging gauge symmetry, the authors demonstrate that the system can be made resilient to noise that affects both spin and spatial coherence. The architecture is shown to be highly flexible, allowing for the 'stacking' of systems to implement universal quantum gates and even a quantum adder using only local, short-range interactions. This approach provides a potential path toward space-efficient and scalable quantum computing architectures.
main_problem: Developing a quantum error correction (QEC) scheme that exploits Quantum Spatial Distribution (QSD) and gauge symmetry to protect against noise affecting both spin and position states.
main_result: The authors prove that gauge symmetry provides resilience against a unified noise model (spin/position decoherence and dephasing) and demonstrate that QSD allows for scalable, space-efficient quantum gates and adders using only nearest-neighbor interactions.
method: The study utilizes the stabilizer formalism and operator quantum error correction, specifically employing Poulin's stabilizer formalism to implement a subsystem structure with gauge qubits.
model_or_system: A system of particles arranged on nested squares where each particle possesses both a spin (coin) state and a position (spatial) state, encoding Shor's nine-qubit code through superpositions of these states.
key_observables: Stabilizer generators (s0-s5), logical Pauli operators (X-bar, Z-bar), and gauge transformations.
important_parameters: Error probabilities (p) for spin-flip, phase-flip, and tunneling/position-related noise; interaction range limited to nearest and next-nearest neighbors.
main_assumptions: Gate operations are assumed to act exclusively on either spin or position states to reduce circuit depth; the error analysis assumes single-event errors do not involve simultaneous Pauli-X operations on both axes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the physical arrangement of particles on nested squares and the concept of gauge symmetry; Figure 2 illustrates the stabilizer measurement scheme; Figure 3 shows a gate-teleportation circuit for the logical Hadamard gate; Figure 4 displays circuits for Toffoli and quantum adder implementations; Figure 5 shows the CX gate procedure and two-layer architecture.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the QSD architecture and encoding, introduces a unified noise model, proves the error-correcting capabilities provided by gauge symmetry, and concludes by demonstrating the implementation of universal quantum gates and a quantum adder.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum computing as it proposes a novel way to use spatial degrees of freedom (positional states) as a resource for error protection and architectural flexibility.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Three physical particles {p0, p2, p4} and two measurement particles {p1, p3}. We specify four vertices of each square as 00, 10, 11, and 01. (b) Conceptual image of the gauge symmetry [Eqs. (3.1) and (3.2)], where M is any operation in End((C2)⊗9) and g is any gauge operation in the gauge algebra G. The effect that the virtual logical qubit ¯q receives from any operation M acting on the three physical particles depends only on M modulo G generated by the gauge transformations and stabilizer generators in Table 1.

Figure 2: (a) Stabilizer measurement scheme, i.e., measurement of s0-s5 eigenvalues. Be- fore measuring the spin state of ancillary particle p1 (resp. p3) at stage k ∈{0, 1, 2}, the sk (resp. sk+1) eigenvalue is mapped onto this spin state. Here, each mapping consists of submappings, each of which transfers the ZcZxIy, ZcIxZy or XcXxXy eigenvalue of physical particle pj±1 onto the spin state of ancillary particle pj. (b) Procedure for s0 eigenvalue map- ping ((|0⟩c)p1 →(|m0⟩c)p1), where m0 ∈{0, 1} denotes that the eigenvalue of s0 is (−1)m0. Figure 3: Gate-teleportation circuit for the logical Hadamard gate that converts the ¯Z eigenstates of the three-particle system to ¯X eigenstates, and vice versa. Here, the upper line in the left-hand circuit is a three-particle system, and the lower line is a particle (pm; mediator particle) that resides in a square with four vertices placed at a site vertically or horizontally adjacent to the system. Two meter symbols on the upper line each represent an eigenvalue measurement of ZcZxZy for the particle pm, and these outcomes are denoted by (−1)m(i) and (−1)m(ii) (m(i),m(ii) ∈{0, 1}), respectively. Figure 4: (a) Circuit for the Toffoli gate, which applies logical ¯X operator to the top system if and only if both of the other two systems are in the −1 eigenstate of ¯Z. (b) Quantum adder [53–55] that adds two numbers a (= a2a1a0) and b (= b2b1b0) and outputs their sum s = a + b (= s3s2s1s0), where ai, bi, si ∈{0, 1} for 0 ≤i ≤3. The signs of their numbers are denoted by a±, b±, and s±, each taking the value 0 or 1 to indicate positive or negative sign, respectively. Here, cj is defined by c1 := a0b0, cj := aj−1bj−1 ⊕bj−1cj−1 ⊕cj−1aj−1 for 2 ≤j ≤3, and c± := a±b± ⊕a±c3 ⊕b±c3. Similarly, we define s0 := a0 ⊕b0, si := ai ⊕bi ⊕ci for 1 ≤i ≤2, and s3 := a±⊕b±⊕c3.
